key_insight: The paper shows that an LLM can be safely used as a constrained front end for finite element simulation, while all solver logic stays in deterministic, human-written FEniCS templates.
engineering_relevance: This makes natural-language setup of multi-physics solid mechanics models more practical for engineers without letting generated code sit on the critical path.
method: LLM parses prompts into structured JSON
LLM only handles non-catalog Gmsh geometry generation with retry feedback
A deterministic dispatcher selects fixed FEniCS/UFL templates for elasticity, hyperelasticity, elastoplasticity, thermo-mechanics, and phase-field fracture
evidence_signal: On 15 parser prompts, final valid parses reached 100% with 97.1% field-extraction accuracy, and on 10 custom-geometry cases the real LLM-to-Gmsh path achieved 90% final success.
watch_item: The approach depends on a carefully constrained template library and still showed one unrecovered invalid-geometry failure in custom geometry generation.
paper_title: A Constrained Natural-Language Interface for Variational Multi-Physics Finite Element Simulations in FEniCS
